milestone_description: Standout features that elevate RASS beyond a well-engineered RAG system into a genuinely novel, technically impressive platform. These items are high-effort, high-reward additions that demonstrate frontier engineering: adaptive retrieval, local model support, multi-modal understanding, and real-time collaboration.

issues.title: [Phase G] Implement adaptive retrieval with user feedback learning
issues.body:
## Problem

RASS retrieves the same results for the same query every time, regardless of whether users found those results helpful. There is no feedback loop to improve retrieval quality based on actual user behavior. In contrast, production search systems continuously improve by learning from clicks, ratings, and explicit feedback.

Without a feedback loop, retrieval quality plateaus at the quality of the initial tuning.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Capture explicit and implicit feedback
#### Explicit feedback
- Add thumbs-up/thumbs-down buttons to each answer in the frontend
- Add per-citation "Was this helpful?" micro-interaction
- `POST /api/feedback` with payload: `{ chatMessageId, type: 'answer'|'citation', signal: 'positive'|'negative', citationId? }`

#### Implicit feedback
- Track which cited documents the user expands/reads (click events)
- Track how far the user scrolls into the citations panel
- Track follow-up query patterns (rephrasing suggests dissatisfaction)

### 2. Persist feedback in the database
```prisma
model RetrievalFeedback {
  id            String   @id @default(cuid())
  userId        String
  chatMessageId String
  citationId    String?
  feedbackType  String   // positive | negative | click | scroll
  chunkId       String?  // OpenSearch document ID
  documentId    String?
  createdAt     DateTime @default(now())
}
```

### 3. Use feedback to adjust retrieval weights
- Implement a simple feedback-weighted scorer: boost `relevanceScore` for chunks/documents that received positive feedback from this user
- `FeedbackBoostStage` — a retrieval pipeline stage that queries `RetrievalFeedback` for the current user and applies multiplicative boosts

### 4. Personalized reranking
- Build a lightweight "personal relevance" score: if a user frequently cites documents from domain X, boost domain X results in future queries
- Store per-user topic affinity scores (computed from feedback history) in Redis for fast lookup

### 5. A/B testing framework
- Assign users randomly to a control group (no adaptation) or treatment group (feedback-boosted retrieval)
- Track retrieval quality metrics per group in the evaluation harness

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Thumbs-up/thumbs-down UI is present on every answer and persists feedback to the database
- [ ] Click events on citations are tracked as implicit feedback signals
- [ ] `FeedbackBoostStage` is implemented and wired into the pipeline (configurable on/off)
- [ ] For a user who repeatedly upvotes answers from Document A, Document A ranks higher in subsequent queries (demonstrated in an integration test)
- [ ] A/B testing framework assigns users to groups and logs group membership with each query
- [ ] Privacy: feedback signals are scoped to the user's workspace and are purged when the user's data is deleted

issues.title: [Phase G] Add local model support via Ollama for fully offline deployments
issues.body:
## Problem

RASS currently requires OpenAI or Google Gemini API keys for both embeddings and LLM generation. This means:
- The system cannot be used without internet access or in air-gapped environments
- All document content is sent to third-party APIs (a non-starter for many enterprises)
- API costs scale linearly with usage
- Latency depends on external API availability

Local model support via Ollama would make RASS self-contained, private, and cost-free to run.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Add Ollama provider to the embedding service
Ollama exposes an OpenAI-compatible API. Create `embedding-service/src/providers/OllamaEmbeddingProvider.js`:
```js
import { OpenAI } from 'openai';
const client = new OpenAI({ baseURL: 'http://ollama:11434/v1', apiKey: 'ollama' });
export async function embed(texts) {
  const response = await client.embeddings.create({ model: config.EMBEDDING_MODEL, input: texts });
  return response.data.map(d => d.embedding);
}
```

### 2. Add Ollama provider to the rass-engine-service
Create `rass-engine-service/src/providers/OllamaLLMProvider.js`:
- Use the OpenAI client with `baseURL: 'http://ollama:11434/v1'`
- Support streaming via the same SSE mechanism used for OpenAI

### 3. Add Ollama to docker-compose
```yaml
ollama:
  image: ollama/ollama:latest
  volumes:
    - ollama_data:/root/.ollama
  ports:
    - "11434:11434"
  deploy:
    resources:
      reservations:
        devices:
          - driver: nvidia
            count: all
            capabilities: [gpu]
```

### 4. Model pull on startup
- Add a startup script that runs `ollama pull <LLM_MODEL>` and `ollama pull <EMBEDDING_MODEL>` before services start
- Recommended defaults: `llama3.2` (3B) for LLM, `nomic-embed-text` (768d) for embeddings

### 5. Configuration
```yaml
EMBEDDING_PROVIDER: ollama   # openai | gemini | ollama
LLM_PROVIDER: ollama         # openai | gemini | ollama
OLLAMA_BASE_URL: http://ollama:11434
EMBEDDING_MODEL: nomic-embed-text
LLM_MODEL: llama3.2
EMBED_DIM: 768
```

### 6. Documentation
- Add an "Offline / Local Mode" section to `README.md` and `DEPLOYMENT.md`
- Document GPU requirements and CPU fallback performance expectations

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Setting `EMBEDDING_PROVIDER: ollama` and `LLM_PROVIDER: ollama` in `config.yml` enables fully local operation
- [ ] A document can be ingested and a question answered with zero external API calls (verified by network monitoring)
- [ ] Ollama service starts and pulls required models automatically on first run
- [ ] Streaming works correctly with Ollama (SSE stream to frontend is identical to OpenAI behavior)
- [ ] CPU-only mode works (slower, but functional) when no GPU is available
- [ ] Documentation clearly states hardware requirements and expected latency for local mode

issues.title: [Phase G] Implement multi-modal document understanding (images, tables, diagrams)
issues.body:
## Problem

RASS processes text-only content. Real-world documents — especially in medical informatics, research, and legal domains — contain:
- Charts and data visualizations that convey key information
- Tables with structured data that text extraction mangles or omits
- Diagrams and figures with embedded labels
- Scanned PDFs where the content is an image (requires OCR)

Ignoring non-text content means RASS silently misses information that a human reader would extract.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Integrate OCR for scanned PDFs
- Add `tesseract.js` (WASM-based Tesseract OCR) to the embedding service
- Before parsing with `pdf-parse`, detect if the PDF contains images with no text layer using `pdfjs-dist`
- If image-only PDF detected, run Tesseract OCR page by page and use the extracted text
- Store `ocrApplied: true` in `DocumentProvenance`

### 2. Table extraction
- Use `tabula-js` (Node.js wrapper for Tabula Java library) to extract tables from PDFs as CSV
- For each extracted table, create a special chunk type: `{ type: 'table', content: '<csv string>', headers: [...] }`
- Generate a natural-language summary of each table using the LLM: "This table shows..."
- Index both the CSV representation and the natural-language summary

### 3. Image description via vision LLM
- Extract images from PDFs using `pdfjs-dist`
- For images > 1000px wide or containing text annotations, call the vision LLM (GPT-4o or Gemini Vision) to generate a description
- Index the description as a chunk with `type: 'image_description'`

### 4. Frontend rendering
- When a citation points to a `table` or `image_description` chunk, render the cited content differently:
  - Tables: render as an HTML table
  - Image descriptions: render as italicized text with a "[Figure]" badge

### 5. Cost and rate limiting
- Vision LLM calls are expensive; implement a per-workspace image-description quota
- Allow opting out via `VISION_ENABLED: false` in config

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A scanned PDF (image-only) is successfully ingested via OCR and its content is retrievable
- [ ] Tables extracted from a PDF are retrievable in natural-language queries about their content
- [ ] The LLM-generated table summary is returned as context when a user asks about table data
- [ ] Image descriptions are generated for figures > 1000px and indexed as retrievable chunks
- [ ] `VISION_ENABLED: false` skips image processing without affecting text ingestion
- [ ] `DocumentProvenance.chunkingStrategy` includes `ocrApplied`, `tablesExtracted`, and `imagesDescribed` fields

issues.title: [Phase G] Implement knowledge graph extraction and interactive visualization
issues.body:
## Problem

RASS retrieves document chunks in response to queries but has no understanding of how concepts, entities, and relationships within those documents connect to each other. This means:
- Multi-hop queries ("How does X relate to Y?") often fail because the connection spans multiple documents
- There is no way to browse the corpus by topic or entity
- The system cannot reason about relationships between documents

A knowledge graph layer would transform RASS from a document retriever into a true knowledge discovery platform.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Entity and relationship extraction
- After chunking, run each parent chunk through an LLM extraction prompt:
  ```
  Extract all named entities (people, organizations, drugs, diseases, concepts) 
  and relationships between them from the following text. Return JSON:
  { entities: [{name, type, description}], relations: [{subject, predicate, object}] }
  ```
- Store entities and relations in new Prisma models:
  ```prisma
  model Entity { id, name, type, kbId, documentId, chunkId }
  model Relation { id, subjectId, predicate, objectId, kbId, chunkId }
  ```

### 2. Graph-augmented retrieval
- After hybrid search retrieves candidate chunks, look up all entities in those chunks
- Expand candidates by following 1-hop relations in the knowledge graph (fetch entity neighbors)
- Re-rank with the expanded context
- This enables multi-hop answers without multi-hop queries

### 3. Interactive graph API
- `GET /api/knowledge-bases/:kbId/graph` — returns the full entity-relation graph (paginated)
- `GET /api/knowledge-bases/:kbId/graph/entity/:entityId` — returns entity neighbors
- Support filtering by entity type, document, or date range

### 4. Frontend interactive visualization
- Render the knowledge graph using `react-force-graph-2d` (already planned in Phase F)
- Color nodes by entity type
- Edge thickness proportional to relationship frequency
- Double-click a node to query RASS about that entity

### 5. Incremental extraction
- Run entity extraction as an async BullMQ job after ingestion completes
- Show a "Building knowledge graph..." indicator in the document library

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Entity and relation extraction runs after ingestion for all documents in a KB
- [ ] `GET /api/knowledge-bases/:kbId/graph` returns a graph with correct entities and relations
- [ ] Graph-augmented retrieval returns relevant results for a multi-hop query that requires bridging two documents
- [ ] The frontend knowledge graph renders entities as colored nodes and relations as labeled edges
- [ ] Double-clicking an entity node in the graph triggers a pre-filled chat query about that entity
- [ ] Extraction is incremental: adding a new document adds its entities without re-extracting the entire KB

issues.title: [Phase G] Add real-time collaborative annotation and knowledge curation
issues.body:
## Problem

RASS is a read-only system from the user's perspective. Users can ask questions and receive answers, but they cannot:
- Annotate retrieved passages with notes or corrections
- Flag inaccurate or outdated information for review
- Collaboratively curate the knowledge base (e.g., mark certain documents as authoritative)
- Share annotated answers with team members

Collaborative curation would make RASS a living knowledge platform, not just a search tool.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Annotation data model
```prisma
model Annotation {
  id            String   @id @default(cuid())
  chunkId       String   // OpenSearch chunk ID
  documentId    String
  userId        String
  workspaceId   String
  annotationType AnnotationType
  content       String?  // for NOTE type
  createdAt     DateTime @default(now())
  updatedAt     DateTime @updatedAt
  User          User     @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])
}

enum AnnotationType {
  NOTE          // free-text annotation
  FLAG_OUTDATED // marks content as potentially outdated
  FLAG_INCORRECT // marks content as incorrect
  AUTHORITATIVE // marks document as high-trust
  BOOKMARK      // user bookmarks a chunk for later
}
```

### 2. Real-time collaboration with WebSocket
- Add a WebSocket endpoint `ws://localhost:8080/ws/annotations` in mcp-server
- When a user adds an annotation, broadcast it to all workspace members currently viewing that document
- Use the existing `ws` WebSocket library already in `rass-engine-service`

### 3. Annotation-aware retrieval
- Chunks flagged as `AUTHORITATIVE` receive a relevance boost in retrieval
- Chunks flagged as `FLAG_INCORRECT` or `FLAG_OUTDATED` are excluded from retrieval (or ranked last)
- This closes the feedback loop: curation directly improves answer quality

### 4. Annotation UI
- In the citations panel, add an annotation button next to each citation
- A popover allows the user to: add a note, flag as outdated, flag as incorrect, or bookmark
- Annotations from other workspace members appear as avatars on the citation
- Real-time updates: when a colleague adds an annotation, it appears instantly via WebSocket

### 5. Shared annotated answers
- `POST /api/chats/:chatId/share` — creates a shareable link for a specific chat session
- The shared view shows the answer with all annotations visible

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A user can add a note annotation to any citation and it persists in the database
- [ ] Annotations from workspace members appear in real time without page refresh (< 500 ms)
- [ ] Chunks with `AUTHORITATIVE` annotation rank higher in retrieval than equivalently-scored unannotated chunks
- [ ] Chunks with `FLAG_INCORRECT` annotation are excluded from retrieval context
- [ ] A shareable link for a chat session displays the answer and all annotations to non-authenticated viewers
- [ ] WebSocket connection handles graceful reconnection on disconnect
